title: Mint Sauce
subtitle: Fresh mint, white wine vinegar, and a little sugar
excerpt: Mint sauce is the traditional British accompaniment to roast lamb: finely chopped fresh mint leaves, a pinch of sugar, and enough white wine vinegar to make a sharp, pourable sauce. It takes five minutes to make and should be made fresh each time. Bottled mint sauce is an acceptable substitute but lacks the brightness of fresh mint. The vinegar must be white wine vinegar, not malt, which is too aggressive.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: mint sauce recipe. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg #35075: mint sauce with vinegar. Traditional British accompaniment to roast lamb, documented for centuries.

content.text: Fresh mint sauce has a brightness that no bottled version can replicate. The key is

in sugar for a few minutes before adding the vinegar: the sugar draws juice from the mint and concentrates the flavour before the vinegar sharpens the whole thing. Use spearmint rather than peppermint or apple mint; spearmint is the traditional British roast lamb companion.

Place the finely chopped {{mint}} in a small bowl. Sprinkle over {{caster-sugar}} and stir to coat. Leave for 5 minutes. Add {{water}} of boiling water and stir to dissolve the sugar. Add {{white-wine-vinegar}} and stir well. Taste: the sauce should be sharp and fresh with a light sweetness. Adjust the balance with a little more sugar or vinegar. Leave for a further 5 minutes before serving.

rows.fix: Add a little more chopped mint and a pinch more sugar. The sauce should be sharp but not mouth-puckering.
rows.cause: Ratio of vinegar to mint is too high

rows.fix: Make the sauce no more than 2 hours ahead. If the colour matters, make it immediately before serving.
rows.cause: Oxidation once the leaves are cut

content.text: Mint sauce is so thoroughly associated with roast lamb in British cookery that it is difficult to encounter one without the other. The combination is old: mint with lamb appears in British cookery books from the sixteenth century. It is one of the few fresh, sharp condiments on the British Sunday table, and its acidity cuts the richness of the lamb in a way that no other accompaniment does.
